lead: Packing well is the difference between belongings that arrive intact after a long journey and a box of heartbreak at the far end. When your possessions are going to spend hours on the motorway rather than minutes across town, every wrap, fill and label matters more. Drawing on two decades of loading vans in Westerfield bound for every corner of the country, here is exactly how our crews pack a home for the long haul.

sections.h: Why Long-Distance Packing Is a Higher Standard
sections.body:
People are often surprised when we say a long-distance move should be packed to a higher standard than a local one, but the reasoning is simple. On a ten-minute drive across Ipswich, a box that is slightly underpacked or a plate that is wrapped a little thinly will probably survive. On a five or six-hour run up the motorway, with the constant low-level vibration of the road and the occasional firm braking, every weakness in your packing gets tested over and over again. Distance is relentless in a way a short trip never is.

That is why our crews treat long-distance packing as a craft. The aim is for every box to be solid and full, with no empty space for things to slide into, and for every fragile item to be cushioned on all sides so it never touches its neighbour. A well-packed box should feel like a single firm unit when you pick it up, not a bag of loose contents. Get this right and the long journey becomes irrelevant; get it wrong and the miles will find the flaw.

The good news is that the principles are straightforward, and anyone willing to take their time can pack to a high standard. The rest of this guide walks through the materials, the techniques and the order of work that we have refined over thousands of long-distance moves out of Suffolk.

Good packing starts with good materials, and skimping here is a false economy on a long move. Use proper double-walled cardboard boxes rather than the thin supermarket variety, which sag and collapse when stacked tight in a van. Have a range of sizes: small, strong boxes for heavy things like books and records, medium boxes for the bulk of your possessions, and larger boxes only for light, bulky items such as bedding and cushions. Mixing weights into the wrong size box is how backs and boxes both get broken.

Beyond boxes, stock up on plenty of packing paper and bubble wrap for breakables, strong parcel tape, and a couple of thick marker pens for labelling. Wardrobe boxes, with a rail across the top, let clothes travel on their hangers and arrive ready to hang, which is a genuine luxury at the far end of a long move. Furniture blankets and corner protectors guard your larger pieces, though our crews bring these as standard. Old towels, tea towels and jumpers double up nicely as extra padding and save buying more than you need.

Buy a little more than you think you will need. Running out of tape or paper halfway through a Sunday evening, with the move days away, is the kind of avoidable stress we see all the time. Any unused materials are easy to pass on, but mid-pack shortages cost you time you do not have.

Fragiles are where long-distance packing really earns its name, because these are the items most punished by hours on the road. Wrap each piece of crockery and glassware individually in packing paper, then stand plates on their edge rather than stacking them flat, as they resist pressure far better that way. Nestle glasses and cups in a box with plenty of paper between and around them, and never leave a gap; fill every void with scrunched paper so nothing can shift, because movement is what breaks things on a long trip.

Mirrors and framed pictures travel best wrapped in bubble wrap, taped, and stood upright on their edge against a firm surface, never laid flat where weight can land on them. Lamps should have their shades removed and packed separately, with the bases wrapped and cushioned. For electricals, the original boxes are ideal if you kept them; if not, wrap them well and pack them snugly. Photograph the back of your television and hi-fi before unplugging, so reconnecting at the other end is quick and stress-free.

Anything genuinely precious or irreplaceable, jewellery, important documents, small heirlooms, should not go on the van at all. Pack these into a bag that travels with you in your own car. On a long move, where you are separated from the van for hours, keeping your most valuable items close gives real peace of mind, and it is what we advise every single customer to do.

The calmest way to pack a whole house is to work room by room, and to start with the rooms you use least. Weeks before the move, tackle the loft, the spare room, the study and out-of-season clothes, because these can be boxed up and set aside without disrupting daily life. Leave the kitchen, the bathroom and the everyday bedroom items until the final days, so you keep living comfortably right up to the move. Working in this order spreads the effort and stops the whole thing landing in one panicked weekend.

As you fill each box, keep the weight sensible. A box you cannot comfortably lift is a hazard to whoever loads it, and on a long move our crews are lifting all day, so a houseful of overloaded boxes slows everything down. Put heavy items in small boxes and light items in big ones, the opposite of what people instinctively do. Seal the bottom of every box with tape running both ways, because a base that gives out under load is a disaster waiting to happen.

Keep a simple inventory as you go, numbering boxes and jotting down roughly what is in each. On a long-distance move, where your belongings are out of sight for hours, an inventory lets you confirm at the far end that everything has arrived. It takes a few extra minutes per box and saves a great deal of worry later.

Labelling is the quiet hero of a long-distance move, and it is worth doing properly. Write on the side of each box, not the top, because once boxes are stacked in the new home you can only read the sides. Note the room the box belongs to and a quick summary of the contents, and mark anything fragile clearly so it gets handled with extra care and loaded sensibly. A consistent system, the same words for the same rooms, makes the whole process flow.

This labelling pays off at both ends. In Suffolk, it helps our crews load the van logically, keeping each room's boxes together and placing fragiles where they will not be crushed. At the destination, it lets us carry every box straight to the room it belongs in, following your labels, which turns unpacking from a treasure hunt into a tidy, room-by-room task. After a long day on the road, having the boxes land in the right rooms is a real gift to yourself.

Consider a colour code on top of the words, a coloured sticker or marker per room, so even a glance tells everyone where a box should go. Pop a matching colour on the door of each room at the new house and the crew can place things without asking. It is a small habit that makes a big difference, particularly on a long move where everyone is tired by the time the van is unloaded.

sections.h: The Essentials Box and Storage Considerations
sections.body:
However well you pack everything else, the most important box on a long-distance move is the one you keep with you. Pack an essentials box, or two, with everything you will need for the journey and your first night before the rest of the house is unpacked: phone chargers, medication, toiletries, a change of clothes, the kettle, a few mugs and tea bags, basic tools, and snacks. Keep it in your own car, not on the van, so that even if your belongings arrive the next morning you are not left without the basics on night one.

It is also worth thinking, as you pack, about whether some of your belongings might be better off in storage for a while. Long-distance moves often involve a gap between completion dates, and if you suspect that might happen, pack and label as if for a normal move but flag the items you will want to access in the meantime. We've helped many Suffolk families keep their things securely with us during that in-between period, then delivered once the new home was ready.

If you are downsizing or renovating, storage lets you move in stages rather than forcing everything into the new place at once. Mention it when we survey your home so we can plan it in, and pack accordingly. Done thoughtfully, the combination of a well-stocked essentials box and a sensible storage plan means even a long, complicated move feels under control from the first box to the last.

faqs: Do I really need stronger boxes for a long-distance move?

Yes. The constant vibration of a long motorway journey tests boxes far more than a short local drive, so thin supermarket boxes simply aren't up to it. Use double-walled boxes, fill them completely so nothing shifts, and keep the weight sensible. A solid, full box survives the miles; an underpacked one rarely does.
How should I pack fragile items so they survive the journey?

Wrap each piece individually, stand plates on edge rather than stacking them flat, and fill every gap with scrunched paper so nothing can move. Mirrors and pictures travel upright on their edge, never flat. Movement is what breaks things on a long trip, so the goal is a box where nothing can shift at all.
Should valuables go on the van?

No. Jewellery, important documents and small irreplaceable items should travel with you in your own car. On a long move you're separated from the van for hours, so keeping your most precious things close gives genuine peace of mind. We advise this for every customer.
Can you pack the house for me?

Absolutely. Our crews offer a full or partial packing service, and a professionally packed home travels a long distance far better. We bring all the materials, pack to a high standard with everything labelled and inventoried, and you can simply leave the most time-consuming part of the move to us.
